title: Your First API Call
description: Get started with the Claude Messages API — from authentication to making your first request and understanding the response format.
learningObjectives: Understand the Claude Messages API structure
Parse and work with the API response format

sections.title: The Messages API
sections.content:
The Claude Messages API is Anthropic's primary interface for integrating Claude into your applications. Every interaction with Claude through the API follows a simple pattern: you send a list of messages and get a response back.

The API endpoint is:
```
POST https://api.anthropic.com/v1/messages
```

Every request you send must include three required fields. The **model** field specifies which Claude model to use, such as `claude-sonnet-4-20250514`. The **max_tokens** field sets a ceiling on how many tokens Claude can generate in its response — this is required because it prevents runaway responses and helps you manage costs. Finally, the **messages** field contains an array of conversation messages that form the input to Claude.

Beyond these three essentials, several optional fields give you finer control over Claude's behavior. The **system** field accepts a system prompt that shapes how Claude approaches its responses — think of it as stage directions for the conversation. The **temperature** field controls the randomness of Claude's output, where 0 produces near-deterministic responses and 1 encourages more creative variation. You can also set **stream** to true if you want to receive the response token by token rather than waiting for the complete output.

To authenticate your requests, pass your API key in the `x-api-key` header. This key identifies your account and is the only credential you need to start making calls.

adaptedExamples.developer: Think of the Messages API like any REST endpoint you integrate with: you POST a JSON body with your model choice, token limit, and conversation messages, authenticate via a header, and get structured JSON back. The pattern is identical to calling Stripe or Twilio.

sections.title: Constructing a Request

adaptedExamples.developer: The messages array works like a chat log your app maintains. For a multi-turn feature like a support chatbot, you append each user message and Claude response to the array before sending the next request — giving Claude the full conversation context your application has tracked.

sections.title: Understanding the Response
sections.content:
When Claude finishes processing your request, the API returns a structured JSON response. Let's look at what comes back:

```json
{
  "id": "msg_abc123",
  "type": "message",
  "role": "assistant",
  "content": [
    {
      "type": "text",
      "text": "Hello! How can I help you today?"
    }
  ],
  "model": "claude-sonnet-4-20250514",
  "stop_reason": "end_turn",
  "usage": {
    "input_tokens": 25,
    "output_tokens": 12
  }
}
```

The **content** field is an array of content blocks. In most straightforward interactions, this array contains a single text block, but it can include multiple blocks when Claude uses tools.

The **stop_reason** field tells you why Claude stopped generating text, and it deserves careful attention. A value of `"end_turn"` means Claude finished its response naturally. If you see `"max_tokens"`, Claude hit the token ceiling you set and the response may be incomplete. The value `"stop_sequence"` indicates Claude encountered a custom stop sequence you defined, and `"tool_use"` means Claude is requesting to call a tool.

Finally, the **usage** field reports how many input and output tokens the request consumed, which is essential for tracking costs and monitoring your API usage.

One best practice worth highlighting: always check the `stop_reason` before using the response. If it's `"max_tokens"`, your output was truncated. In that case, you should either increase your `max_tokens` value or instruct Claude to be more concise so the full answer fits within the limit.

adaptedExamples.developer: Always check stop_reason before using the response in your application logic. If your parsing code expects complete JSON but stop_reason is max_tokens, the output is truncated and JSON.parse() will throw — handle this case explicitly in your error handling.

sections.title: Design an API Request
sections.content: Now it's time to put these concepts into practice by constructing a complete API request for a realistic use case.

exercise.prompt:
You're building a feature that summarizes customer feedback emails into structured data. Write out the complete API request body (as JSON) that you would send to the Claude Messages API. The request should:

1. Use an appropriate model
2. Include a system prompt that instructs Claude to extract: sentiment (positive/negative/neutral), main topics (array), urgency level (low/medium/high), and a one-line summary
3. Include a sample user message with a fictional customer email
4. Set appropriate max_tokens

Write the full JSON request body.
hints: Choose a model that balances cost and capability — claude-sonnet-4-20250514 is a good default
Your system prompt should specify the exact JSON output format you want
Include an example of what the output should look like in the system prompt
Think about what max_tokens you need for a structured JSON response
exercise.evaluationCriteria: The response should be a valid JSON request body with: (1) a model field with a real Claude model name, (2) a system prompt that clearly defines the extraction task and output format, (3) a messages array with at least one user message containing a realistic customer email, (4) reasonable max_tokens (256-1024 for this task). Bonus for including temperature (low for structured extraction) and specifying JSON output format in the system prompt.

sections.title: Check Your Understanding
sections.content: Think about the API concepts you've just learned.

exercise.prompt: You're building a multi-turn chatbot. After the user sends their third message, the API returns a response with stop_reason: "max_tokens". What happened, what are the consequences, and how would you fix it?
exercise.evaluationCriteria: Should explain that: (1) Claude's response was truncated because it hit the max_tokens limit, (2) the user received an incomplete response, (3) solutions include increasing max_tokens, instructing Claude to be more concise in the system prompt, or implementing a continuation strategy where you send the truncated response back and ask Claude to continue. Bonus for mentioning that in multi-turn conversations, the accumulated context (all previous messages) counts toward understanding why this might happen later in a conversation.

sections.title: Key Takeaway
sections.content: The Claude Messages API follows a simple request-response pattern: send messages, get a response. Every request needs three things — a model, a max_tokens limit, and a messages array. Use system prompts to shape Claude's behavior for your specific use case, and always check the stop_reason in the response to confirm you received a complete answer rather than a truncated one.
